Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study when I felt like it, which wasn't that often because most of the material just didn't grab me. I'm a hands-on learner, so sitting and reading or watching videos feels tedious. Usually I'd cram things in whenever I remembered or when something was due soon. I know I should be more organized, but I just couldn't bring myself to care enough to set up a proper schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and pull everything together in one intense session. It really depended on what else was going on in my life and whether I could muster up the energy to deal with the anxiety of logging in. Regular study times just never stuck for me.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting an email reminder that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed. It wasn't like I ever felt excited to go on there—it was more like a chore I kept putting off until I absolutely couldn't anymore. The gamified tasks helped a little because at least they were slightly less boring.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to a point where logging in felt automatic or natural. It always felt like this thing I had to force myself to do. The closest I got was during the gamified task period when there were points and badges involved—that made it slightly more tolerable. But even then, it never became a habit I looked forward to.
Q5: The practice problems and anything interactive were the most useful because I could actually do something instead of just passively absorbing information. The gamified elements helped too because at least there was some sense of progress and achievement. Videos were okay if they were short, but I'd zone out if they went on too long. I need to be physically engaged to really learn.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums entirely. I never posted anything meaningful and I had zero peer interactions because the thought of putting myself out there like that made my anxiety spike. The longer reading materials I'd skim at best or just download and never look at again. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade, it probably got ignored.
Q7: It had to feel like it would actually help me on assignments or exams, or it had to be at least somewhat engaging. The gamified stuff worked because there was immediate feedback and a sense of accomplishment. If something felt like busywork or if I couldn't see the point, I'd just skip it. My time is limited and my motivation is even more limited, so I had to be selective.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it—simple as that. Required activities at least had the threat of affecting my grade, which was enough to push through the boredom and anxiety. Optional stuff just felt like extra stress I didn't need, even if it might have been helpful. I already had enough on my plate.
Q9: Yeah, a lot actually. I'd download PDFs and practice problems and work through them on paper or on my tablet offline. I'm kinesthetic, so writing things out by hand helps me remember better than clicking through screens. I also used some YouTube videos and external resources that explained things in ways that made more sense to me. The LMS wouldn't show any of that.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be working through problems in a notebook or reviewing printed materials for hours, but my LMS activity would show nothing. I'd also study with downloaded content during my commute or in places without good internet. Some of my best studying happened completely disconnected from the platform because I could focus better without the distractions.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture slides or readings once, then never open them on the LMS again. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without needing to be online. It also meant I didn't have to deal with the LMS interface, which honestly stressed me out sometimes. So my actual engagement with materials was way higher than my login data would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially when my anxiety was really bad. Sometimes just the thought of logging in and seeing all the things I hadn't done yet would paralyze me. When I was feeling particularly bored or disconnected from the material, it was almost impossible to make myself engage. Those periods could last days, and I'd fall behind on attendance.
Q13: Absolutely. When I had other things going on—work, personal stuff, just feeling overwhelmed—the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. The fatigue was real; sometimes I was just too mentally exhausted to deal with another screen. Competing responsibilities meant I'd prioritize things with more immediate consequences, and coursework would slide until it became urgent.
Q14: Oh, definitely. There were times I'd be logged in, scrolling through content, but my brain was completely elsewhere. I'd watch videos without really processing anything or click through readings just to mark them complete. It felt like going through the motions without actually learning. Those sessions were basically useless even though they'd show up as activity.
Q15: I procrastinated on discussion posts constantly because the social aspect made me anxious. I'd also put off starting assignments until the last possible moment, even knowing they were important. The anxiety of potentially doing something wrong combined with the boredom of the content created this perfect storm of avoidance. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become the day before it was due.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd have these bursts of intense activity right before something was due, then go quiet again. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure was the only thing that could cut through my boredom and anxiety. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all. My whole semester was basically a series of last-minute scrambles.
Q17: Getting good feedback was actually motivating—it reminded me I could do this even when I didn't feel like it. But if feedback was delayed or vague, it just added to my anxiety and made me less likely to engage with similar tasks. The gamified task feedback was nice because it was immediate. Knowing I was doing well helped push through the boredom a bit.
Q18: The structure was kind of a double-edged sword. Having clear deadlines helped because I need external pressure to function. But the amount of content and the expectation to engage regularly didn't match how I actually learn best. I would have done better with more hands-on projects and fewer passive activities. The weekly rhythm didn't work for my sporadic engagement style.
Q19: The gamified elements made engagement easier because they added some variety and made things feel less like a chore. But the heavy reliance on videos and readings made it harder because that's not how I learn best. The discussion requirements were the worst—they felt forced and increased my anxiety significantly. More flexibility in how to demonstrate learning would have helped.
Q20: I think some students might be like me—logging in, clicking around, trying to engage but not actually absorbing anything. Or they could be anxious about submitting work that isn't perfect, so they keep reviewing without ever finishing. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes being on the LMS is just procrastination disguised as studying.
Q21: That's literally me. My exam scores and assignment grades are high even though my LMS activity looks pretty low. I do a lot of studying offline, and I'm efficient when I actually sit down to work. The LMS doesn't capture that I can learn quickly when I focus—it just shows I wasn't logging in regularly. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when people are active and what they click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone was actually learning or just going through motions. They also miss all offline studying, mental health struggles, and the difference between productive and unproductive time online. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through content—it's being present and invested. For me, real engagement happens when I'm doing something active, like working through problems or applying concepts. Passive consumption doesn't feel like engagement to me, even if it looks like it on paper.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I dealt with just trying to participate, or how bored I was with most of the content despite performing well. It wouldn't capture my offline studying or the fact that I learn best by doing, not watching. It also wouldn't show that my low engagement wasn't about not caring about learning—it was about the format not working for how my brain operates.
